title: The Civil Rights Movement expands
description: Practice your skills in this exercise about the expanded Civil Rights Movement.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “If the police do not protect homosexuals as they have not protected us in the past, then I can see in the near future a separate police force paid for and operated by the homophile community. Unless restrictive laws are changed, unless the courts uphold the rights of homosexuals, we shall have no alternative but to go to the Supreme Court and overturn these laws that state all men are treated equally in our courts except for homosexuals. If politicians do not openly address themselves to homosexuals, it will be because they do not need our 90,000 votes in San Francisco. We shall put in office public servants who will talk to homosexuals. 

> “We ask no special favor. We want only ordinary rights like every other citizen of these United States— jobs, homes, friends, social lives, safety and security. 

> “Here is our challenge to San Francisco: FACE REALITY—FACE HOMOSEXUALITY.”

>-William Beardemphl, “A Challenge to San Francisco,” *The Ladder,* 1966

**This excerpt is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: mobilization for legal and social equality by lesbian and gay activists
persecution of suspected homosexuals in public sector positions
emergence of a health crisis that primarily affected homosexual men
introduction of federal legislation prohibiting same-sex marriage

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“If the police do not protect homosexuals as they have not protected us in the past, then I can see in the near future a separate police force paid for and operated by the homophile community. Unless restrictive laws are changed, unless the courts uphold the rights of homosexuals, we shall have no alternative but to go to the Supreme Court and overturn these laws that state all men are treated equally in our courts except for homosexuals. If politicians do not openly address themselves to homosexuals, it will be because they do not need our 90,000 votes in San Francisco. We shall put in office public servants who will talk to homosexuals. 

> “We ask no special favor. We want only ordinary rights like every other citizen of these United States— jobs, homes, friends, social lives, safety and security. 

> “Here is our challenge to San Francisco: FACE REALITY—FACE HOMOSEXUALITY.”

>-William Beardemphl, “A Challenge to San Francisco,” *The Ladder,* 1966

**Arguments similar to those expressed in the excerpt were later employed to justify which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the legalization of same-sex marriage
the ratification of the Twenty-sixth Amendment
the formation of the Civil Rights Commission
the introduction of Title IX protections

Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “If the police do not protect homosexuals as they have not protected us in the past, then I can see in the near future a separate police force paid for and operated by the homophile community. Unless restrictive laws are changed, unless the courts uphold the rights of homosexuals, we shall have no alternative but to go to the Supreme Court and overturn these laws that state all men are treated equally in our courts except for homosexuals. **The ideas of the Gay and Lesbian Rights Movement, as expressed in the excerpt, had most in common with the ideas of which of the following groups?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Black Power movement
the Populist movement
the American Indian movement
the anti-war movement

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“If the rules to settle our economic problems are not forthcoming, we will call a general strike to paralyze the state’s agricultural economy. [applause] We will call a general strike to let the legislators and the employers know that we mean business. We will take economic pressure, strikes, boycotts, to force recognition and obtain collective bargaining rights. The social and economic revolution of the farm workers is well underway and it will not be stopped until we receive equality. The farm workers are moving. Nothing is going to stop them.” 

>-Dolores Huerta, march and rally in Sacramento, [transcript from University of California San Diego,](https://libraries.ucsd.edu/farmworkermovement/media/oral_history/ParadigmArchive/arc%2029.pdf) 1966

**The excerpt best serves as evidence for which of the following developments in the 1960s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: new demands for labor protections for Latino Americans
efforts to include bilingual education in public schools
increased conflict over unequal pay for men and women
calls to reinstate the *Bracero* program for needed labor

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“If the rules to settle our economic problems are not forthcoming, we will call a general strike to paralyze the state’s agricultural economy. [applause] We will call a general strike to let the legislators and the employers know that we mean business. We will take economic pressure, strikes, boycotts, to force recognition and obtain collective bargaining rights. The social and economic revolution of the farm workers is well underway and it will not be stopped until we receive equality. The farm workers are moving. Nothing is going to stop them.” 

>-Dolores Huerta, march and rally in Sacramento, [transcript from University of California San Diego,](https://libraries.ucsd.edu/farmworkermovement/media/oral_history/ParadigmArchive/arc%2029.pdf) 1966

**The tactics described in the excerpt best represent which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: using nonviolence
practicing self-defense
lobbying politicians in office
damaging federal property

Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

**Which of the following movements used tactics most similar to those expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the civil rights movement
the conservation movement
the education reform movement
the anti-war movement

assessment_items.question_content:
> “But it’s no good— deep in our guts we know this. Cooking and cleaning and children have not given us the fulfillment the ladies’ magazines promise even after we’ve followed all their recipes. . . . Some women— such as those who have expressed themselves in the platform of this march— look to the state and federal legislation to give us the unrestricted humanity which has been denied us for so long. They have decided to ‘work within the system.’ . . . Is this really what we want? How about female generals in Vietnam? DO WE WANT EQUALITY IN THE MAN’S WORLD, OR DO WE WANT TO MAKE IT IN  A NEW WORLD?

> “Women being ourselves and believing in ourselves, women finding the strength to live how we feel, *powerful* women, can lead the way to create a new kind of politics, a new life.” 

>-Source: Bread and Roses, *Outreach Leaflet,* 1970

**Which of the following types of evidence supports the argument in the excerpt that some women looked “to the state and federal legislation to give us ... unrestricted humanity”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: supporting civil rights laws to end discrimination in higher education
filing legal challenges to laws that discriminate based on gender
reducing federal funding for Planned Parenthood locations
nominating the first female candidates to Congress

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Women’s Liberation movement
the suffragette movement
the temperance movement
the conservation movement

assessment_items.question_content:
> “But it’s no good— deep in our guts we know this. Cooking and cleaning and children have not given us the fulfillment the ladies’ magazines promise even after we’ve followed all their recipes. . . . Some women— such as those who have expressed themselves in the platform of this march— look to the state and federal legislation to give us the unrestricted humanity which has been denied us for so long. They have decided to ‘work within the system.’ . . . Is this really what we want? How about female generals in Vietnam? DO WE WANT EQUALITY IN THE MAN’S WORLD, OR DO WE WANT TO MAKE IT IN  A NEW WORLD?

> “Women being ourselves and believing in ourselves, women finding the strength to live how we feel, *powerful* women, can lead the way to create a new kind of politics, a new life.” 

>-Source: Bread and Roses, *Outreach Leaflet,* 1970

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how feminists rejected the previous generation’s social roles
how women’s gender roles changed after Women’s Liberation
how the conservative movement affected second-wave feminism
how the Women’s Liberation movement divided along racial lines

assessment_items.question_content:
> “You say, ‘the basic misperception is that our enemy is man, not capitalism.’ I say, the basic misperception is the facile identification of the ‘system’ with ‘capitalism.’ In reality, the American system consists of two interdependent but distinct parts— the capitalist state and the patriarchal family. . . .

> “But it is important for women to recognize and deal with the exploited position in the family  system for it is primarily in terms of the family system that we are oppressed *as women.* If you really *think* about our exploitation under capitalism— as cheap labor and as consumers— you will see that our position in the family system is at the root.”

>-Source: Ellen Willis, “Letter to the Left,” *The Guardian,* 1969

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how women challenged the social and economic values of the previous generation
how women maintained the political values of their parents’ generation
how women became the primary base for the Republican Party
how women fought to expand the role of government to intervene in the economy

assessment_items.question_content:
> “You say, ‘the basic misperception is that our enemy is man, not capitalism.’ I say, the basic misperception is the facile identification of the ‘system’ with ‘capitalism.’ In reality, the American system consists of two interdependent but distinct parts— the capitalist state and the patriarchal family. . . .

> “But it is important for women to recognize and deal with the exploited position in the family  system for it is primarily in terms of the family system that we are oppressed *as women.* If you really *think* about our exploitation under capitalism— as cheap labor and as consumers— you will see that our position in the family system is at the root.”

>-Source: Ellen Willis, “Letter to the Left,” *The Guardian,* 1969

**The excerpt was most likely intended to do which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to encourage men in the left-leaning political parties to address gender issues and sexism
to persuade women to leave the Democratic Party and form their own political party
to convince women to stop marrying men and becoming mothers to keep them from oppression
to urge men to take on more household chores within their families to reduce women’s subjugation

assessment_items.question_content:
> “You say, ‘the basic misperception is that our enemy is man, not capitalism.’ I say, the basic misperception is the facile identification of the ‘system’ with ‘capitalism.’ In reality, the American system consists of two interdependent but distinct parts— the capitalist state and the patriarchal family. . . .

> “But it is important for women to recognize and deal with the exploited position in the family  system for it is primarily in terms of the family system that we are oppressed *as women.* If you really *think* about our exploitation under capitalism— as cheap labor and as consumers— you will see that our position in the family system is at the root.”

>-Source: Ellen Willis, “Letter to the Left,” *The Guardian,* 1969

**Which of the following was a key difference between First-wave feminism of the 1900s and Second-wave feminism of the 1960s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: First-wave feminists fought to gain political rights, while second-wave feminists aimed to change social roles and access to employment.
First-wave feminists influenced politics at the state level, while second-wave feminists influenced politics at the local level.
First-wave feminists were largely unsuccessful, while second-wave feminists achieved all of their goals.
First-wave feminists were mainly middle-class women, while second-wave feminists were mainly women of color.
